topic: A student finds out their scholarship was meant for someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, official letter lay on my desk, the glossy paper mocking me with its perfect typography. My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming beneath my ribs. I traced the name listed at the top—a name that wasn't mine. Every word swam before my eyes. I reread the paragraph detailing the financial aid, the precise wording about the intended recipient. A slow, burning heat started creeping up my neck, making my skin prickle. I picked up the phone, my hand trembling slightly as I dialed the scholarship committee. When the secretary confirmed the details, the sound of her voice felt distant, muffled, as if I were speaking through thick glass. "I... I need to understand," I managed, the words sounding thin and reedy. The silence that followed felt vast, echoing the hollowness spreading through my chest.

[story 2]

I stared at the acceptance email, my jaw tight enough to ache. It was supposed to be mine. The sheer audacity of the situation made my hands shake so badly I had to grip the edge of the wooden table. I felt a sudden, sharp wave of nausea wash over me. Who was this person they had chosen? The realization hit me like a physical blow, stealing the air from my lungs. I pushed the screen away, leaning back until the chair squeaked in protest. My breathing hitched, shallow and fast. When I finally spoke to the advisor, my voice came out unnervingly steady, though I could feel the tremor running through my limbs. "This isn't right," I stated, the tone flat, devoid of any warmth. I waited, bracing myself for the inevitable explanation, but the air in the room felt suddenly too thin to breathe.

[story 3]

The envelope felt heavy, a physical weight pressing down on my chest. Opening it was like watching a carefully constructed dam break. The names, the awards, the funding amount—it was all there, meticulously laid out, but the designated beneficiary was clearly someone else entirely. A profound sense of wrongness settled over me, heavy and suffocating. I dropped the letter onto the floor, the sound sharp in the quiet library. I stood up abruptly, pacing the narrow aisle between the towering bookshelves. My gaze darted around the room, searching for an explanation, but found only indifferent stacks of knowledge. A flush rose across my cheeks, hot and undeniable. I couldn't look at anyone, afraid that if I did, the reality of the deception would shatter something inside me completely.
